title: DevOps & Cloud Mastery Quiz
description: Expert questions on Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, CI/CD, GitOps, monitoring, and cloud architecture.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a Docker image and a Docker container?
options: A. Image is a read-only layered template; container is a running instance of that image with a writable layer
B. Container is a read-only template; image is the running instance
C. They are the same thing stored in different locations
D. Image is stored in memory; container is stored on disk

questions.question: In a multi-stage Dockerfile, what does the '--target build' flag accomplish?
options: A. Names the final image 'build' in the local registry
B. Stops the build at the stage named 'build', skipping subsequent stages
C. Enables parallel execution of all stages up to 'build'
D. Copies only the 'build' stage artifacts to the host

questions.question: Which Docker run flag makes the container filesystem read-only, improving security posture?
options: A. --no-write
B. --immutable
C. --read-only
D. --ro-filesystem

questions.question: In Kubernetes, what is the role of the 'etcd' component?
options: A. Schedules pods onto worker nodes based on resource availability
B. Routes traffic between pods using iptables rules
C. Manages container lifecycle on each worker node
D. Distributed key-value store — the single source of truth for all cluster state

questions.question: What does a Kubernetes Deployment's 'maxUnavailable: 0, maxSurge: 1' rolling update strategy guarantee?
options: A. One extra pod is created before any old pod is removed — capacity never drops below desired replicas
B. All pods are replaced simultaneously with zero downtime
C. Updates pause after each pod for manual approval
D. Only one pod runs at any time during the update

questions.question: What is the difference between a Kubernetes Service type 'ClusterIP' and 'LoadBalancer'?
options: A. ClusterIP exposes to internet; LoadBalancer is internal only
B. LoadBalancer provisions an external cloud load balancer; ClusterIP is only reachable within the cluster
C. ClusterIP is for TCP; LoadBalancer is for HTTP only
D. LoadBalancer auto-scales pods; ClusterIP does not

questions.question: What Kubernetes object ensures exactly one pod instance runs on every node in the cluster?
options: A. Deployment with replicas matching node count
B. StatefulSet
C. DaemonSet
D. ReplicaSet

questions.question: In Terraform, what is stored in the '.terraform.lock.hcl' file?
options: A. The encrypted state of all managed resources
B. Variable values for the current workspace
C. The plan output from the last 'terraform plan' run
D. Provider version constraints and checksums — ensures reproducible provider installations across team members

questions.question: Which Terraform command imports an existing AWS S3 bucket into state management WITHOUT recreating it?
options: A. terraform import aws_s3_bucket.existing my-bucket-name
B. terraform apply -target=aws_s3_bucket.existing
C. terraform state add aws_s3_bucket.existing my-bucket-name
D. terraform refresh -resource=aws_s3_bucket.existing

questions.question: What does 'terraform validate' check that 'terraform fmt' does not?
options: A. fmt checks syntax errors; validate only formats code
B. validate checks configuration correctness and internal consistency (valid references, required args); fmt only fixes code formatting
C. They check identical things — validate is just slower
D. validate compares state with cloud reality; fmt does not

questions.question: In GitOps with ArgoCD, what does 'self-heal: true' do?
options: A. Automatically fixes application bugs using AI analysis
B. Restarts crashed containers without human intervention
C. Reverts manual changes made directly to the cluster that drift from the Git-declared state
D. Heals broken Git merge conflicts automatically

questions.question: In GitHub Actions, which permission is required to request an OIDC token for keyless cloud authentication?
options: A. contents: write
B. packages: write
C. security-events: write
D. id-token: write

questions.question: What does pinning a GitHub Action by full SHA (e.g., uses: actions/checkout@abc1234...) protect against?
options: A. Supply chain attacks where a tag is moved to point to malicious code — SHA is immutable
B. Protects against the action becoming slow over time
C. Ensures the action only runs on Ubuntu runners
D. Prevents breaking changes from newer action versions

questions.question: What Prometheus function should you use on a counter metric to get requests per second?
options: A. increase()
B. rate()
C. delta()
D. avg_over_time()

questions.question: In the Google SRE Golden Signals framework, which signal is most directly tied to user experience?
options: A. Saturation — how full the service is
B. Traffic — how much demand exists
C. Latency — time to serve a request (the user waits for this)
D. Errors — rate of failed requests

questions.question: What is the primary security benefit of using Workload Identity (GKE) or IRSA (EKS) instead of environment variable credentials in pods?
options: A. Credentials are automatically rotated every 30 minutes instead of daily
B. Cloud provider encrypts the environment variables at rest
C. Access is audited by the Kubernetes API server rather than CloudTrail
D. No static long-lived credentials are stored anywhere — pods receive short-lived tokens via OIDC federation, reducing blast radius of credential theft

questions.question: What does 'immutable infrastructure' mean in practice?
options: A. Servers that cannot be modified after deployment — changes require replacing the server entirely with a new image, eliminating configuration drift
B. Infrastructure running on read-only filesystems
C. Infrastructure that is permanently hosted and never decommissioned
D. Containers that use read-only Docker images

questions.question: In Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, what happens by default if no NetworkPolicy selects a pod?
options: A. All ingress and egress traffic to/from the pod is blocked
B. All ingress and egress traffic to/from the pod is allowed
C. Ingress is blocked; egress is allowed by default
D. The pod is automatically placed in a quarantine namespace

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Kubernetes PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)?
options: A. Limits CPU and memory spending per pod
B. Controls pod scheduling priority relative to other pods
C. Ensures a minimum number of pods remain available during voluntary disruptions like node drains or rolling updates
D. Sets cost budgets for cloud resources consumed by pods

questions.question: Which Helm command upgrades a release but rolls back automatically if the deployment fails?
options: A. helm upgrade --force myrelease ./chart
B. helm upgrade --wait --timeout 300s myrelease ./chart
C. helm rollout upgrade myrelease ./chart
D. helm upgrade --atomic myrelease ./chart

questions.question: What AWS component allows private access to S3 from within a VPC without traffic traversing the internet?
options: A. VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3
B. NAT Gateway
C. Transit Gateway
D. PrivateLink Interface Endpoint

questions.question: In a CI/CD pipeline, what is a 'canary deployment' and what metric triggers rollback?
options: A. Deploy to a test environment only; rollback if tests fail
B. Route a small percentage of traffic to the new version; monitor error rate and latency — if SLO thresholds are breached, roll back automatically
C. Deploy to one availability zone first; rollback if health checks fail
D. A blue/green deployment with a canary test user group

questions.question: What is the purpose of an SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) in DevSecOps?
options: A. A billing document for cloud resource usage
B. A manifest file listing all Kubernetes resources in a namespace
C. A complete inventory of all software components, libraries, and their versions in an artifact — enables rapid CVE impact assessment
D. A bill of Terraform resources to be provisioned

questions.question: In Kubernetes, what does 'kubectl rollout undo deployment/api' roll back to?
options: A. The initial deployment when the Deployment was first created
B. A specific revision number must be provided — the command alone fails
C. Clears all rollout history and resets to the chart default
D. The previous revision — the last known-good ReplicaSet before the current one

questions.question: What does the Ansible 'handlers' section in a playbook do?
options: A. Defines tasks triggered by 'notify' — run once at the end of a play only if notified, useful for restarting services only when config changes
B. Defines tasks that always run, regardless of whether changes occurred
C. Handles errors and provides fallback tasks for failures
D. Manages inventory host groups and connection handlers
